query: Long-run instrument behavior gives the spread. A fresh run of 81 readings has average 10.42. Put a two-sided uncertainty interval around the true center.
A dozen values look roughly bell-shaped. Only their average and sample spread are reported. Give the interval for the underlying center.
Before collecting data, the lab wants the final mean interval to have half-width below a tolerance, and the historical sigma is available. How many observations are needed?
The question is about the population spread itself, not the center. The data are normal and only s squared and n are given. Build the interval.
A tail area under the null model is 0.018. Explain what this says and what it does not say about the null.
A rejection cutoff is fixed before data arrive. Now evaluate the chance the rule fires when the true mean has shifted.
In quality screening, a good batch may be rejected and a bad batch may pass. Name the two risks and relate them to the rule boundary.
The observed evidence is weak at the chosen level. Write the conclusion without treating the default model as proven.
Only the surprising results from a large family of attempted tests are shown in the report. Diagnose the inferential problem.
The production target is 50 and the process sigma is known from calibration. A large sample average is above target. Decide whether the target has been exceeded.
A small normal sample is compared to a claimed center. The spread must be estimated from the same sample. Decide whether the center is lower than claimed.
The contract limits variability. A normal sample gives a sample spread that seems too large. Test whether the process variation violates the limit.
The distribution is very asymmetric, and the claim concerns the middle observation in the population. Count observations on each side of the claimed value.
The paired differences are centered by ranking their absolute sizes, with signs restored afterward. Use the rank sums to judge a location shift.
The one-sample differences are not normal but look reasonably symmetric. Use a rank-based alternative that still uses magnitudes.
A binary response is observed in one random sample. Estimate the unknown success chance and check whether the normal approximation is acceptable.
A campaign claims at least 60 percent support. One survey gives a smaller fraction. Test the claim using the null standard error.
The observed number of successes is near the boundary, so the symmetric normal interval leaves the parameter space. Diagnose the method.
Two independent groups produce yes/no outcomes. Decide whether the success chances differ, using a combined estimate under equality.
Two independent binary samples are summarized by x1/n1 and x2/n2. Estimate the difference in success probabilities without pooling.
Two normal machines are compared only through their sample variances. Decide whether the first machine is more variable.
Give an interval for the ratio of two normal population variances, paying attention to which sample variance is in the numerator.
Two unrelated normal groups have a common spread by assumption. Compare their centers using one combined spread estimate.
A two-group mean comparison uses sp squared. Explain how the common variance estimate enters the denominator.
Two unrelated groups have very different reported spreads and unbalanced sample sizes. Compare their centers without sharing a variance estimate.
The denominator has s1 squared over n1 plus s2 squared over n2, and the degrees of freedom are approximate. Identify the procedure.
Each unit appears twice in the spreadsheet. Analyze the changes within units, not the two columns as independent groups.
The treatment comparison uses the same subjects under both conditions. State the correct sampling unit and test type.
Two unrelated small samples are ordinal and the normal model is doubtful. Replace measurements by ranks to compare locations.
Two quantitative measurements are taken on each subject. Test whether the linear association parameter is zero.
The sample association is far from zero, but inference is requested on the population correlation scale. Use the variance-stabilizing transform.
Observed counts in categories are compared with a fully specified probability vector. Decide if the distributional model is plausible.
The category probabilities come from a fitted one-parameter model before computing the count discrepancy. Adjust the degrees of freedom.
Rows are exposure groups and columns are outcomes. Expected counts are built from margins to decide if the variables are associated.
A rectangular count table has r rows and c columns. State the null model and the degrees of freedom for association testing.
A line is fit to predict y from one x. Decide whether the fitted tilt could be sampling noise.
Given Sxx and Sxy, recover the fitted line and interpret the change in fitted response per unit x.
At a chosen x value, estimate the average response for all units at that x, not a new individual.
At a chosen x value, the target is one future observed y. The interval must include both mean uncertainty and noise.
Several y values are observed at each x level. Split the residual sum of squares into repeatability error and systematic curvature.
The residual plot bends and fans out. State what is wrong with the straight-line error model.
Write the model for y as X times a coefficient vector plus errors, and identify the projection that creates fitted values.
Some rows of the design matrix are unusual. Use the diagonal of the projection matrix to discuss leverage.
A table decomposes response variation into regression and error for several predictors. Test whether the predictors help jointly.
With other predictors retained, decide whether one coefficient is different from zero using its estimate and standard error.
A smaller model is nested in a larger one. The question is whether the extra block of variables reduces error enough.
A categorical factor is encoded into regression columns. Explain the baseline group and how coefficients shift intercepts.
The effect of x is allowed to differ by group through a product term. Interpret the group-specific slopes.
A candidate with slightly worse in-sample fit predicts deleted observations better. Use the validation-style statistic to choose a model.
A search over many predictors gives impressive in-sample R squared but poor deleted-residual performance. Diagnose the model-selection issue.

A filling machine has known population standard deviation 0.18 ounces. From 49 bottles the sample mean is 15.94. Construct a 95 percent confidence interval for the true mean fill and state whether 16 ounces is plausible.
A random sample of 16 tensile strengths from an approximately normal population has mean 72.4 and sample standard deviation 5.6. Find a 99 percent confidence interval for the population mean.
A test statistic gives p-value 0.032 for H0. Explain the evidence against the null at the 5 percent and 1 percent levels, without saying the null is probably true or false.
Before sampling, a critical region is chosen so alpha is 0.05. For a practically important alternative, compute or describe beta and the power of the test.
A researcher runs many tests and reports only the significant p-values. Explain why this NHST workflow is misleading and what fail to reject should mean.
A process mean is claimed to be 100. The population standard deviation is known to be 12. A sample of 64 has mean 103.5. Test the one-sided alternative that the mean is larger.
A normal population has unknown variance. Ten observations give xbar 8.7 and s 1.9. Test H0 mu equals 10 against H1 mu less than 10 at alpha 0.05.
A normal measurement process is required to have standard deviation at most 2.0. A sample of 21 gives s squared equal to 6.8. Test whether the variability exceeds the requirement.
A skewed lifetime distribution has claimed median 500 hours. In 18 independent items, 14 last longer than 500. Use a nonparametric sign test for the median claim.
Out of 220 randomly selected components, 29 are defective. Test whether the defect probability is below 0.18 using a large-sample normal approximation.
Two independent production lines have 18 defects among 150 and 31 defects among 170. Test whether the two defect proportions differ.
Two independent normal samples give n1 14, s1 squared 9.4 and n2 12, s2 squared 3.1. Test whether the population variance of group 1 is larger.
Two independent normal samples appear to have a common variance. Use the pooled two-sample t statistic to test whether mu1 minus mu2 is zero.
Two independent samples have noticeably unequal sample variances and unequal sample sizes. Test whether the mean response in treatment A is greater without pooling variances.
The same 12 machines are measured before and after calibration. Test whether the average before-after difference is nonzero using the paired differences.
For bivariate normal data with n equal to 25 and sample correlation r equal to 0.52, test whether the population correlation is positive.
A die is rolled 120 times and the six face counts are recorded. Test whether the die follows the specified uniform probabilities.
A two-way table classifies employees by department and job satisfaction level. Test whether department and satisfaction are independent categorical variables.
At four x levels there are replicated y observations. Use pure error and lack-of-fit sums of squares to decide whether a straight line is an adequate regression mean.
In a multiple regression model with five predictors, test whether adding two predictors significantly reduces error after the other three predictors are already in the model.

A quality-control report gives sigma = 3.2 from long-run calibration data. A new batch of 64 observations has average 41.7. Give the 95 percent interval estimate for the process mean and comment on whether 40 is consistent with the data.
Only nine observations are available from a normal population. Their mean is 12.6 and sample standard deviation is 2.4. Estimate the population mean with a two-sided 90 percent confidence interval.
A statistician reports a p-value of 0.004 when testing a null model. State what this means as evidence and give the decision at alpha equal to 0.01.

For observations from a normal model with known variance, a rule rejects H0 only if the sample mean exceeds a cutoff chosen before seeing data. Find the cutoff for alpha 0.05 and describe power at a larger mean.
A huge sample makes a tiny difference statistically significant. Explain why this does not automatically imply practical importance, and what the test conclusion should and should not say.
A supplier says its normal process variance is at least 25. A sample of 18 gives s squared 14.2. Test whether the variance has fallen below the claimed value.
The differences from a paired experiment are roughly symmetric but not assumed normal. Use a signed-rank approach to test whether the median difference is zero.
A strongly skewed distribution has claimed center 30. In a sample, count how many observations fall above and below 30, and use that count to test the median claim.
No pilot estimate is available, and the desired margin of error for estimating a population proportion is 0.03 at 95 percent confidence. Choose the conservative sample size.
Two independent surveys report 81 yes responses out of 300 and 97 yes responses out of 350. Decide whether the two population proportions are different and state the large-sample test statistic form.
For two independent normal populations, the sample variances are 11.6 and 4.8 with sample sizes 13 and 16. Build a confidence interval for the ratio sigma1 squared over sigma2 squared.
Two independent normal samples have similar spreads, and the problem explicitly says to assume equal variances. Compare the two population means with the appropriate t procedure.
A treatment group has n=11, s=8.2 and a control group has n=31, s=2.9. The problem asks for a confidence interval for the difference in means without a common variance assumption.
Each patient is measured on blood pressure before and after a drug. Analyze whether the mean change is negative, making clear what the observational unit is.
Two independent small samples have ordinal pain scores and normality is doubtful. Compare the locations using ranks rather than the raw measurements.
A bivariate-normal sample gives r=0.61 with n=28. Use the Fisher transformation to form inference about the population correlation.
Counts fall into five categories and are compared with probabilities from a model after estimating one parameter from the same data. Set up the chi-square goodness-of-fit test and degrees of freedom.
A contingency table records treatment group by outcome category. Compute expected counts from row and column totals and test whether treatment and outcome are associated.
Given Sxx, Sxy, xbar and ybar for a simple linear regression, compute the fitted intercept and slope and interpret the slope in context.
After fitting a straight-line regression, construct an interval for one future response at x star and explain why it is wider than the mean-response interval.
There are repeated observations at each of several dose levels. Decompose error into pure error and lack of fit to test whether the linear mean function is adequate.
Write the multiple regression model in matrix form, identify X, beta, and the hat matrix, and state the least-squares estimator.
A regression ANOVA table gives SSR and SSE for four predictors. Test whether the model with all predictors explains significant variation in the response.
Compare a reduced regression model to a full model that adds three predictors. Use the extra sum of squares idea to test whether the added variables are useful.
A regression uses a dummy variable for material type and an interaction between material type and temperature. Explain how the slopes and intercepts differ between groups.
A lab wants a two-sided confidence interval for a normal mean with known sigma = 6 and half-width no more than 1.5 at 95 percent confidence. Determine the required sample size.
A 90 percent confidence interval for a mean is reported as [18.2, 21.7]. Give the correct repeated-sampling interpretation and identify the common wrong probability interpretation.
A small p-value is obtained in a significance test. Explain, in Fisher's sense, what kind of evidence it provides against the null hypothesis.
For a medical screening rule, describe Type I error, Type II error, and how increasing the rejection region would change alpha and beta.
A p-value is 0.18 at alpha 0.05. Write the conclusion carefully, avoiding the statement that the null hypothesis has been proven true.
A normal sample of size 25 has sample mean 102 and sample standard deviation 10. Find a 95 percent lower confidence bound for the population mean.
For a normal population, n = 20 and s squared = 12. Construct a confidence interval for the population variance using chi-square critical values.
Nonzero paired differences are ranked by absolute value and the positive ranks sum to W plus. Use W plus and W minus to perform a Wilcoxon signed-rank test.
A sample has n = 70 and x = 4 successes. A student uses the usual Wald interval for p. Diagnose whether the success-failure condition supports the normal approximation.
Two independent samples have x1 = 44 of n1 = 200 and x2 = 66 of n2 = 240. Construct a confidence interval for p1 minus p2 and state whether zero is plausible.
Two normal samples have s1 squared smaller than s2 squared. Explain how to arrange the variance ratio for an F test and how the degrees of freedom follow the numerator and denominator.
Before applying a pooled two-sample t test, state the assumptions about independence, normality, and equality of variances, and explain the role of sp squared.
Independent samples have unequal standard deviations. Use the Satterthwaite degrees of freedom idea to compare the means without assuming a common variance.
A study measures each student's score under two teaching methods. Explain why this is a paired design rather than two independent samples and choose the correct test.
For paired quantitative observations from a bivariate normal population, test H0 rho equals zero using the sample correlation coefficient.
A goodness-of-fit table has several expected counts below 5. Explain what should be done before trusting the chi-square approximation and how degrees of freedom may change.
In simple linear regression, use SST, SSR, and SSE to compute R squared and explain what proportion of variation is explained by the fitted line.
For a fitted simple regression line, construct a confidence interval for the expected response at x0, not for a future individual observation.
In a multiple regression output, test whether beta3 equals zero while the other predictors remain in the model, using the estimated coefficient and its standard error.
Two candidate regression models have similar R squared, but one has a smaller PRESS statistic on deleted observations. Explain how PRESS is used for model selection and prediction.
